cards.q: Which option best completes this essay point? "...the evidence as I see it and say who does and doesn’t seem to benefit, but this next section is less of a “_____” and more of a guide of what’s worth bringing up to a healthcare provider."
cards.e: I can lay out the evidence as I see it and say who does and doesn’t seem to benefit, but this next section is less of a “try this at home” and more of a guide of what’s worth bringing up to a healthcare provider.

cards.q: Why does the essay say this means melatonin tends to work best when it’s used to shift or reinforce that internal circadian clock, not as a…?
Fill in the blank: "Someone more prone to expectation effects or _____ effects may knock out quickly, but that’s not the melatonin."
cards.e: Someone more prone to expectation effects or placebo effects may knock out quickly, but that’s not the melatonin.

cards.q: Why does the essay say that’s where the problem is,?
cards.a: Most people are popping melatonin for general or transient insomnia, which is really a misuse of it.

cards.q: What does the essay say about combo overactive thoughts sleep?
cards.a: A combo of overactive thoughts and poor sleep hygiene led me to melatonin, an easy answer that was natural and available easily over the counter (so I thought).

decks.t: Vitamin D, Dementia, and Rhonda Patrick’s Claims

cards.q: Which option best completes this essay point? "By _____ nearly follows the same trend over and over, she gives the impression that the causal link between vitamin D and dementia in the overall population is settled, but it’s not."
cards.e: By acting like the evidence nearly follows the same trend over and over, she gives the impression that the causal link between vitamin D and dementia in the overall population is settled, but it’s not.

cards.q: Which option best completes this essay point? "Saying vitamin D “_____” makes it sound like a causal guarantee, but that’s not what the evidence says."
cards.e: Saying vitamin D “reduces dementia risk by 40%” makes it sound like a causal guarantee, but that’s not what the evidence says.

cards.q: Fill in the blank: "So, with that said, let’s look _____ really shows us and how a little too much optimism can quietly slip into the realm of misinformation."
cards.e: So, with that said, let’s look at what the evidence really shows us and how a little too much optimism can quietly slip into the realm of misinformation.

cards.q: Which option best completes this essay point? "I’d bet, dollars to donuts, that they were also more likely to see a doctor regularly, follow their directions, and exercise more, all of which would contribute to a _____ of dementia."
cards.e: I’d bet, dollars to donuts, that they were also more likely to see a doctor regularly, follow their directions, and exercise more, all of which would contribute to a decreased risk of dementia.

cards.q: What does the essay say about dementia and evidence?
cards.a: If those people have higher rates of dementia, despite everything else being equal, we get some stronger evidence that low vitamin D might be causing dementia.

cards.q: Which option best completes this essay point? "One paper found that genetically predicted vitamin D deficiency of below 10ng/mL was associated with a 54% _____ of dementia."
cards.e: One paper found that genetically predicted vitamin D deficiency of below 10ng/mL was associated with a 54% increased risk of dementia.

cards.q: Which option best completes this essay point? "One of the largest to date had over 170,000 participants (28k of which had serum vitamin D measured in the form of 25(OH)D) and found no causal link between vitamin D and _____ 4 ."
cards.e: One of the largest to date had over 170,000 participants (28k of which had serum vitamin D measured in the form of 25(OH)D) and found no causal link between vitamin D and dementia 4 .

cards.q: Fill in the blank: "Rhonda Patrick explaining how vitamin D supplements can _____ by 40% 2 ."
cards.e: Rhonda Patrick explaining how vitamin D supplements can cut your dementia risk by 40% 2 .

cards.q: What does the essay say about dementia?
cards.a: The video highlights a large 2023 study of over 12,000 adults without dementia at baseline.

cards.q: Which option best completes this essay point? "When a well-known science communicator begins to blur the _____ and settled fact, they risk misleading the very same people they set out to help."
cards.e: When a well-known science communicator begins to blur the lines between early evidence and settled fact, they risk misleading the very same people they set out to help.

decks.t: Creatine Benefits and Evidence

cards.q: Which option best completes this essay point? "A recent _____ article framed creatine as a trendy, potentially serious supplement for brain health, bone density, biological aging, sleep, and even mood."
cards.e: A recent Wall Street Journal article framed creatine as a trendy, potentially serious supplement for brain health, bone density, biological aging, sleep, and even mood.

cards.q: Which option best completes this essay point? "If you have _____, high blood pressure, bipolar disorder, or you’re pregnant or breastfeeding , you should avoid it or talk to your doctor before supplementing."
cards.e: If you have kidney or liver disease, high blood pressure, bipolar disorder, or you’re pregnant or breastfeeding , you should avoid it or talk to your doctor before supplementing.

cards.q: Fill in the blank: "The warning earlier is more in line with the conservative view the medical establishment has against recommending something that hasn’t been tested in a _____."
cards.e: The warning earlier is more in line with the conservative view the medical establishment has against recommending something that hasn’t been tested in a specific population.

cards.q: Which option best completes this essay point? "A few days before that I came across a twitter thread by Brady Holmer (titled _____) offering an extremely thoughtful take on the topic, arguing that creatine is safe, helpful, but wildly..."
cards.e: A few days before that I came across a twitter thread by Brady Holmer (titled Creatine Is Overrated) offering an extremely thoughtful take on the topic, arguing that creatine is safe, helpful, but wildly overhyped.

cards.q: What does the essay say about brain?
cards.a: So while the internet continues to debate whether creatine helps you get that extra rep in at the gym or do some cognitive brain game faster, I want to zoom out a bit.

cards.q: Which option best completes this essay point? "Vegetarians/vegans, women, older adults, those of us on the ‘_____’, and those who eat minimal or avoid red meat altogether."
cards.e: Vegetarians/vegans, women, older adults, those of us on the ‘Standard American Diet’, and those who eat minimal or avoid red meat altogether.

cards.q: Which option best completes this essay point? "But they’re _____ to show the true potential of creatine."
cards.e: But they’re not the best population to show the true potential of creatine.

cards.q: Fill in the blank: "Improving strength by a few percent at 75 years old might mean standing up without help _____ of a fall."
cards.e: Improving strength by a few percent at 75 years old might mean standing up without help or reducing the risk of a fall.

cards.q: What caveat does the essay make about you’re already eating saturation?
cards.a: If you’re already eating a lot of red meat, you may be near that saturation point and further supplementation, while not risky, may be rather pointless.

cards.q: Which option best completes this essay point? "It’s a supplement with plenty of upside and no established downside in _____ in a healthy population."
cards.e: It’s a supplement with plenty of upside and no established downside in terms of side effects in a healthy population.
